Code fragment: public final TableField<BudgetRecord, Float> AMMOUNT = createField(DSL.name("ammount"), SQLDataType.REAL.nullable(false), this, "");
Explanation: The 'ammount' column uses SQLDataType.REAL (floating-point), which can cause precision loss when storing monetary values like budget amounts. Change the column type to DECIMAL or NUMERIC with appropriate precision and scale (e.g., DECIMAL(19,4)) to ensure accurate financial calculations.

Code fragment: public final TableField<BudgetRecord, Float> ALLTRANSACTIONAMMOUNT = createField(DSL.name("allTransactionAmmount"), SQLDataType.REAL, this, "");
Explanation: The 'allTransactionAmmount' column uses SQLDataType.REAL (floating-point), which can introduce rounding errors when summing transaction amounts. Use DECIMAL or NUMERIC with appropriate precision and scale to maintain accuracy in financial calculations.

Code fragment: public final TableField<BudgetRecord, LocalDateTime> CREATED_AT = createField(DSL.name("created_at"), SQLDataType.LOCALDATETIME(0).defaultValue(DSL.field(DSL.raw("CURRENT_TIMESTAMP"), SQLDataType.LOCALDATETIME)), this, "");
Explanation: The 'created_at' column has a default value but is still marked as NULLABLE, which is inconsistent since a creation timestamp should never be NULL in practice. Add .nullable(false) to the column definition to enforce that this column always has a value.

Code fragment: public final TableField<TransactionsRecord, Float> AMMOUNT = createField(DSL.name("ammount"), SQLDataType.REAL.nullable(false), this, "");
Explanation: The 'ammount' column uses REAL (floating-point) type for storing monetary values, which can lead to precision loss and rounding errors in financial calculations. Change the column type to DECIMAL or NUMERIC with appropriate precision and scale (e.g., DECIMAL(19,4)) to ensure accurate monetary calculations.

Code fragment: public final TableField<UserRecord, String> SALT = createField(DSL.name("salt"), SQLDataType.VARCHAR(256), this, "");
Explanation: The 'salt' column is marked as NULLABLE, but a cryptographic salt should always be present when a password exists. Consider making this column NOT NULL and ensuring it is always generated alongside the password.
